html:
Peter Ulrich Weiss, born on November 8, 1916, in Nowawes (now part of Potsdam), Germany, and died on May 10, 1982, in Stockholm, was a German-Swedish author, book and magazine illustrator, graphic artist, painter, and director.

== Biography == Peter Weiss was the son of Eugen and Franziska Weiss (née Hummel). His father was a textile manufacturer, and his mother had been an actress and came from Switzerland. Weiss also had a younger brother named Alexander, who later became a writer as well. His father came from Galicia and had converted from Judaism. The family lived in Przemyśl in Galicia in 1917 but then moved back to Potsdam and subsequently to Bremen. In 1929, the family moved again, this time to Berlin. In 1934, the family emigrated to Chislehurst in England. They then moved to Warnsdorf in Bohemia (now part of the Czech Republic), where his father was offered a job. When Nazi Germany annexed the Sudetenland, the family fled to Sweden. His father had contacts within the textile industry and became managing director at Silfa Fabriks AB in Alingsås, and also founded Eugen Weiss Textile Printing. Silfa developed into Scandinavia's largest film printing company. His father held a patent for textile film printing. Peter Weiss spent some time in Switzerland and Prague before joining his family in Alingsås, where they lived in Villa Lillgården. In 1939, he lost his Czechoslovakian citizenship. Weiss has described his time in Alingsås in his works.

Weiss left the family home and moved to Stockholm to work as an artist. He became a Swedish citizen in 1946. In the early 1950s, Peter Weiss was involved in experimental and documentary short film projects. He directed the experimental feature film "Hägringen" and participated in the production of the commercial feature film "Svenska flickor i Paris" alongside Barbro Boman. After "Hägringen" failed at the box office, he received an art commission and decorated the sky bar in the Wenner-Gren Center in Stockholm.

He achieved international recognition with the play "Jean Paul Marat persecuted by the enemies of equality will be put on trial soon," which is more commonly known as "Marat/Sade" or "The Assassination of Jean-Paul Marat." This play was performed in theaters around the world, including a production directed by Peter Brook. Later plays include "The Investigation Concerning Auschwitz," "Trotsky in Exile," and "Hölderlin."

In 1982, Peter Weiss, together with his wife Gunilla Palmstierna-Weiss, directed their own play, "Nya processen" (The New Trial), at the Royal Dramatic Theatre (Dramaten) with Stellan Skarsgård in the lead role. He had signed a contract to write three new plays for Dramaten, but he passed away two months after the premiere of the first play. "Nya processen" therefore became his last play.

== Family == Weiss was married three times: from 1943 to 1947 to Helga Henschen, from 1949 to 1951 to Carlota Dethorey, and from 1964 until his death to Gunilla Palmstierna-Weiss. Peter Weiss had four children: one daughter with Henschen and one son with Dethorey. With Gunilla Palmstierna-Weiss, he had a daughter named Nadja Weiss, and they also adopted Palmstierna-Weiss's son. He is buried at the Northern Cemetery.

== Bibliography (published in Swedish), selected titles == (Titles marked with an asterisk were originally written in Swedish; the others are in German.)

Peter Weiss [exhibition catalogue] (Gummesons konsthall, 1945) From Island to Island (Bonniers, 1947) * The Defeated (Bonniers, 1948) * Document. 1 (Björkmans eftr., 1949) * The Duel (Björkmans eftr., 1953) Avant-Garde Film (Wahlström & Widstrand, 1956) Diagnosis (translation by Benkt-Erik Hedin, Cavefors, 1963) Walking for Three Voices (translation by Benkt-Erik Hedin, Cavefors, 1964) Focus (translation by Benkt-Erik Hedin, Cavefors, 1964) The Inquisition: Oratorio in 11 Songs (translation by Britt G. Hallqvist, Cavefors, 1965) Jean Paul Marat, Persecuted and Murdered, as Depicted by the Inmates of the Charenton Hospital Under the Direction of Monsieur de Sade (translation by Britt G. Hallqvist, Cavefors, 1965) The Shadow of the Coachman's Body (translation by Kjell Ekström, Tiden, 1966) Song of Skråpuken (Gesang vom Lusifanischen Popanz) (translated by Etienne Glaser [and others], Cavefors, 1967) Night with Guests (translated by Peter Weiss, Staffan Lamm, Cavefors, 1967) Reports (translation by Lars Gustafsson [and others], Cavefors, 1968) Notes on Cultural Life in the Democratic Republic of Vietnam (translation by Vanja Lantz, Cavefors, 1969) Trotsky in Exile (translation by Karin Johannisson, Cavefors, 1970) Hölderlin, Play in Two Acts (translation by Britt G. Hallqvist, Norstedts, 1972) The Aesthetics of Resistance (translation by Ulrika Wallenström, Arbetarkultur, 1976–1981) Published Posthumously Notebooks (translation by Ulrika Wallenström, Arbetarkultur, 1984–1985) Landscapes in Dreams: From the Workbooks of a Young Author (translation by Ulrika Wallenström, Gidlund, 1991) Convalescence Diary (translation by Ulrika Wallenström, Bonniers, 1993) [diary and "dream book" written after Weiss suffered a heart attack in 1970] Situation (Bonniers, 2001) [written in 1956]

== Prizes and Awards == 1950 – Recipient of the Boklotteriet scholarship 1956 – Recipient of the Boklotteriet scholarship

How certain are we about this?

People from the 20th century are extremely well-documented. Uncertainty rarely concerns the facts themselves, but rather the interpretation and evaluation of those facts, which can change over time and depending on the perspective.
tldr: Peter Weiss was a German-Swedish artist, writer, and director who became world-renowned for his dramatic plays.
lattlast:
Peter Weiss was born in Germany in 1916 and moved to Sweden during the 1930s due to the war. He initially lived in Alingsås, where his father ran a large company specializing in textile printing, before moving to Stockholm to work as an artist and actor.

He gained international recognition with the play "Marat/Sade," which was performed in theaters around the world. He also wrote several other well-known works exploring themes of history and politics. During his time in Sweden, he worked in film, visual arts, and theater.

Before his death in 1982, he co-directed the play "Nya processen" at the Royal Dramatic Theatre in Stockholm with his wife. He was a prolific writer who authored numerous books and texts that were translated into several languages.
